description: Lock the mid-sixteenth-century mathematical instrument-making trade while avoiding an anachronistic uniform scientific-instrument guild.

checks.reason: Oxford and trade-history sources place commercial mathematical instrument making in London in the 1550s.
London makers used existing livery-company structures for apprenticeship and legal trade status.
Several guilds hosted makers; no single dedicated scientific-instrument guild constituted the whole trade.
The trade produced instruments embodying practical mathematical scales and procedures.
The workshop trade remains upstream of formal instrument calibration practices.
